revised_claim: In the first-century Mediterranean world, the oikos was the basic unit of social organization, and Paul's letters operated within Greco-Roman household conventions. Bruce Malina and John J. Pilch's social-scientific commentary analyzes the honor/shame framework within which Pauline instructions operated. The household codes (haustafeln) appear primarily in Ephesians and Colossians; for those, deSilva's treatment of honor/patronage dynamics is the more directly applicable source.
revised_source: Bruce Malina and John J. Pilch, *Social-Science Commentary on the Letters of Paul*, Fortress, 2006; David deSilva, *Honor, Patronage, Kinship and Purity*, IVP Academic, 2000
response_note: I misidentified the co-author as Rohrbaugh rather than Pilch in Round 1, and the household code material falls partly outside the 2006 volume's scope; the haustafeln argument now rests on deSilva, whose treatment of Mediterranean honor dynamics and household structure is directly relevant.

[CITATION_RESPONSE: R1_SA_003]
[CORRECTION_RECEIVED: NEEDS_HUMAN_REVIEW]
[ADVOCATE_POSITION: QUALIFY]

The household codes (haustafeln) appear primarily in Ephesians and Colossians; for those, deSilva's treatment of honor/patronage dynamics is the more directly applicable source.]
[REVISED_SOURCE: Bruce Malina and John J. Pilch, *Social-Science Commentary on the Letters of Paul*, Fortress, 2006; David deSilva, *Honor, Patronage, Kinship and Purity*, IVP Academic, 2000]
